docs.title: Létourneau-Guillon Lab
docs.text:
Welcome to the internal wiki of the Létourneau-Guillon research lab at the CRCHUM, Université de Montréal.

Our work focuses on neuroradiology AI and medical image segmentation, with a particular interest in intracranial hemorrhage and stroke imaging.

docs.title: Quick links
docs.text:
Team Meet the lab members Research Ongoing projects and publications Resources Learning resources by topic Onboarding Getting started at the CRCHUM Contact How to reach us

New to the lab?

Start with the Onboarding page, then explore Resources.

docs.title: Onboarding
docs.text:
Bienvenue dans le laboratoire du Dr Laurent Létourneau-Guillon au CRCHUM. Cette page regroupe les informations essentielles pour bien démarrer votre stage ou projet de recherche.

Page inspirée du laboratoire NeuroPoly.

docs.title: Arrivée au CRCHUM
docs.text:
1. Formulaires d'inscription et contrat — Compléter avec le CRCHUM. Personne ressource : melissa.nyembo.chum@ssss.gouv.qc.ca

  2. Carte d'accès — Après avoir rempli le formulaire d'inscription, se présenter à la sécurité au 1er étage (apporter une pièce d'identité).

  3. Accès informatique — Compte pXXXXXX et jeton VPN pour l'accès à distance. Contact : simon.lessard.chum@ssss.gouv.qc.ca

docs.title: Formations éthiques obligatoires
docs.text:
Exigence réglementaire

Ces formations sont obligatoires au CRCHUM. Vous devez obtenir votre code pXXXXXX avant de pouvoir accéder aux modules en ligne (sauf le module CITI).

  • Consulter le document listant les formations à compléter (demander à votre superviseur).
  • Conserver une copie d'attestation pour chaque formation et la déposer dans votre dossier étudiant.

docs.title: Google Drive — Projets
docs.text:
Données confidentielles

Aucune information ou donnée confidentielle ne doit être déposée sur ce disque.

Accès au Google Drive partagé (lien à mettre à jour)

docs.title: Pourquoi Google Drive vs OneDrive/Microsoft ?
docs.text:
Avantage Détail Google Docs en ligne Plus rapide que l'équivalent Microsoft en ligne Intégration Zotero Compatible avec Google Docs (non disponible dans Word Online) Google Colab Pratique pour les notebooks partagés

Désavantage : Idéalement les données devraient être sur le serveur UdeM — migration possible vers OneDrive.

Alternative pour manuscrits : Overleaf (LaTeX avec gestion des références)

docs.title: Types de contenu autorisés
docs.text:
• Résultats d'analyses désidentifiés (tableaux, figures finales)
  • Manuscrits (rédigés dans Google Docs avec l'extension Zotero)
  • Données de jeux de données publiques (accès ne peut pas être mis sur "everyone")
  • Code synchronisé depuis GitHub

docs.title: Multiple Sclerosis Lesion Imaging
docs.text: An introduction to neuroimaging anatomy and MS lesion imaging for new lab members.

docs.title: What is MS?
docs.text: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic inflammatory demyelinating disease of the central nervous system (CNS). MRI is the cornerstone of diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment evaluation. Lesions appear as focal areas of demyelination and axonal loss, most visible on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences.

docs.title: MRI Sequences and What They Show
docs.text:
Sequence Appearance of MS Lesions Key Use T2-w Hyperintense (bright) Detect all lesions FLAIR Hyperintense (bright); CSF suppressed Best for periventricular and cortical lesions T1-w (post-Gd) Enhancing = active/acute lesion Inflammation, blood-brain barrier breakdown T1-w (pre-Gd) Hypointense "black holes" Chronic axonal loss DWI Variable; restricted diffusion in acute phase Differentiate from acute stroke

Start here for sequence basics

  • MRI Masterclass — T1 vs T2 vs PD vs FLAIR (interactive)
  • Radiology Masterclass — T1 vs T2 (free tutorial)
  • MRI Masterclass — FLAIR physics and MS pathology

docs.title: MS Lesion Locations
docs.text:
MS lesions follow characteristic topographic patterns. The McDonald criteria use four locations for dissemination in space (DIS):

Location Notes Periventricular Along lateral ventricles; "Dawson's fingers" perpendicular to ventricles on sagittal FLAIR Juxtacortical/cortical Touching the cortex; may be cortical or leuco-cortical Infratentorial Brainstem and cerebellum Spinal cord Cervical > thoracic; typically peripheral and < 2 vertebral segments Optic nerve (2024 criteria) Fifth site added in 2024 revision

Dawson's Fingers

Periventricular lesions oriented perpendicular to the lateral ventricles, following medullary veins. A hallmark of MS. Radiopaedia — Dawson's Fingers

docs.title: McDonald Diagnostic Criteria (2024)
docs.text:
The McDonald criteria define MS diagnosis using dissemination in space (DIS) and dissemination in time (DIT).

The 2024 revision (Lancet Neurology) introduced:

  • Optic nerve as a 5th topographic DIS site
  • CSF biomarker (kappa free light chains) as a substitute for DIT
  • Central vein sign (CVS) and paramagnetic rim lesions (PRL) as optional high-specificity markers
Resource Link Lancet Neurology 2024 (abstract) Link ECTRIMS 2025 Slide Deck PDF Radiopaedia — McDonald Criteria Link MS Trust plain-language explanation Link

docs.title: Comprehensive Guides
docs.text:
• The Radiology Assistant — MS 2.0 — Illustrated, free; covers McDonald criteria, lesion locations, differential diagnosis, annotated real MRI cases
  • Assessment of MS Lesions on MRI — Brain 2019 (open access) — Practical guidelines for recognizing MS lesions; covers lesion types, red flags, CVS, ring-enhancing lesions
  • StatPearls — Multiple Sclerosis — Free, up-to-date clinical chapter with labeled FLAIR examples

docs.title: Radiopaedia Cases (Free)
docs.text:
• MS — main reference article
  • MS brain and spine case
  • Active MS with enhancing lesions
  • MS with brain atrophy
  • MAGNIMS-CMSC-NAIMS MRI consensus
  • Tumefactive MS

  • Harvard Whole Brain Atlas — normal + pathology MRI
  • Practical Neurology — MS Lesion Checklist (10 lesion types)

docs.title: Public MS MRI Datasets
docs.text:
Open-access brain MRI datasets for MS lesion segmentation — suitable for students learning to train or evaluate segmentation models.

Where to start

If you just want to get started quickly:

  • No registration needed: Mendeley 60-patient dataset or Open MS Data
  • Best annotated benchmark: MSSEG-1 (MICCAI 2016)
  • Longitudinal / new lesions: MSSEG-2 (MICCAI 2021)

docs.title: Dataset Overview
docs.text: Dataset Year Subjects Modalities Access Size ISBI 2015 2015 5 train / 14 test T1, T2, PD, FLAIR (longitudinal) Free (registration) ~82 sessions MSSEG-1 2016 53 FLAIR, T1, T1-Gd, PD-T2 Free (Shanoir) 4 scanners, 7 raters MSSEG-2 2021 100 FLAIR (2 timepoints) Free (Shanoir + DUA) ~6 GB Shifts 2022 2022 Multi-site FLAIR + T1 Free (Zenodo DUA) Train/val/test splits Open MS Data 2015 Cross-sectional + longitudinal co-registered, bias-corrected Free (CC-BY) GitHub Mendeley 60 patients 2022 60 T1, T2, FLAIR Free (CC-BY) 1.5T, NIfTI MSLesSeg 2025 75 (115 scans) T1, T2, FLAIR + clinical Free (registration) Multi-timepoint PediMS 2025 9 (28 scans) T1, T2, FLAIR Free (CC-BY, figshare) Pediatric

docs.title: ISBI 2015 Longitudinal MS Challenge
docs.text:
Longitudinal dataset with two expert rater masks per session — ideal for evaluating longitudinal lesion detection.

  • Subjects: 5 training, 14 test (mean 4.4 timepoints each, 82 MRI sessions total)
  • Modalities: T1, T2, PD, FLAIR (3T, longitudinal)
  • Access: Free after registration
  • Challenge site: smart-stats-tools.org/lesion-challenge-2015
  • IACL data page: iacl.ece.jhu.edu — MSChallenge/data
  • Paper (NeuroImage 2017): PMC

docs.title: MSSEG-1 (MICCAI 2016)
docs.text:
The most widely used MS segmentation benchmark. Multi-site, multi-scanner, 7 expert raters with consensus segmentation.

  • Subjects: 53 patients, 4 scanners
  • Modalities: 3D FLAIR, T1, T1 post-Gd, PD-T2
  • Access: Free via Shanoir-NG (free academic account required)
  • Data portal: portal.fli-iam.irisa.fr — MSSEG
  • Paper (NeuroImage 2021): ScienceDirect | PubMed

docs.title: MSSEG-2 (MICCAI 2021) — New Lesion Detection
docs.text:
Focused on detecting new or enlarging lesions between two timepoints — a clinically crucial task.

  • Subjects: 100 patients from the French OFSEP cohort
  • Modalities: 3D FLAIR (baseline + follow-up)
  • Access: Free via Shanoir-NG after signing Data Usage Agreement (~6 GB)
  • Data portal: portal.fli-iam.irisa.fr/msseg-2
  • Challenge code (ivadomed): github.com/ivadomed/ms-challenge-2021

docs.title: Shifts Challenge 2022
docs.text:
Multi-source dataset combining MSSEG and ISBI data, organized for benchmarking model robustness under dataset shift.

  • Access: Free via Zenodo after signing DUA (CC-BY-NC-SA 4.0)
  • Zenodo Part 1: zenodo.org/records/7051658
  • Zenodo Part 2: zenodo.org/records/7051692
  • Grand Challenge page: shifts.grand-challenge.org
  • GitHub (data readers + examples): github.com/Shifts-Project/shifts/tree/main/mswml

docs.title: Open MS Data (Muschelli)
docs.text:
Pre-processed, co-registered, bias-corrected NIfTI files — good for getting started without heavy preprocessing.

  • Content: Cross-sectional + longitudinal data; CC-BY license
  • Processing: Co-registered to FLAIR, 1×1×1 mm isotropic
  • GitHub: github.com/muschellij2/open_ms_data
  • Tutorial: johnmuschelli.com/neuroc/ms_lesion

docs.title: Brain MRI 60 MS Patients (Mendeley)
docs.text:
Directly downloadable, CC-BY dataset with expert consensus segmentations. No registration required.

  • Subjects: 60 patients (46F/14M, mean age 33), 20 centres, 1.5T, acquired 2019–2020
  • Modalities: T1, T2, FLAIR — each with consensus masks (2 radiologists + 1 neurologist)
  • Format: NIfTI, CC-BY license
  • Download: data.mendeley.com/datasets/8bctsm8jz7/1
  • Paper: PMC

docs.title: MSLesSeg (Scientific Data 2025)
docs.text:
Recent multi-timepoint benchmark with clinical data included.

  • Subjects: 75 patients, 115 scans, 1–4 timepoints (mean age 37 ± 10)
  • Modalities: T1, T2, FLAIR + clinical metadata
  • Paper: Nature Scientific Data 2025

docs.title: PediMS — Pediatric MS
docs.text:
First public MRI dataset specifically for pediatric MS segmentation.

  • Subjects: 9 patients, 28 scans, 1–6 timepoints; CC-BY 4.0
  • Modalities: T1 (MPRAGE), T2, FLAIR + clinical data
  • Download (figshare): figshare.com — PediMS
  • Paper: Nature Scientific Data 2025 | PMC

docs.title: General Neuroimaging Platforms
docs.text:
• OpenNeuro — Broad open-access platform; searchable for MS datasets in BIDS format
  • Kaggle MS Dataset — Good for quick experimentation (free Kaggle login)

docs.title: MS Lesion Segmentation Tools
docs.text: Overview of open-source models for automated white matter lesion segmentation in MS.

docs.title: LST — Lesion Segmentation Toolbox (Classic)
docs.text:
LST is a MATLAB/SPM12 toolbox for automated segmentation of T2-hyperintense white matter lesions, originally developed for MS.

  • Official site: applied-statistics.de/lst.html
  • Documentation (PDF): LST v3.0.0 manual
  • Requires: MATLAB + SPM12

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
1. Download LST_3.0.0.zip from the official site
  2. Unzip into your SPM12 toolboxes folder: <SPM12_root>/toolbox/LST/
  3. Open MATLAB → launch SPM12 → click Toolbox → select LST

docs.title: Algorithms
docs.text:
Algorithm Input Notes LGA (Lesion Growth Algorithm) FLAIR + T1 Original algorithm (Schmidt et al. 2012) LPA (Lesion Prediction Algorithm) FLAIR only Faster; good for large cohorts Longitudinal Longitudinal FLAIR ± T1 For detecting new/enlarging lesions

Additional tools: lesion filling, lesion probability maps, report generation.

docs.title: Key Papers
docs.text:
• Schmidt et al. 2012 — LGA: NeuroImage 59(4):3774–3783
  • Schmidt et al. 2019 — Longitudinal segmentation: see toolbox homepage

docs.title: LST-AI — Deep Learning Extension
docs.text:
LST-AI is a fully open-source deep learning successor to classic LST, developed at TU Munich. It uses an ensemble of three 3D U-Nets with a composite loss (binary cross-entropy + Tversky) to address class imbalance between lesion and non-lesion tissue. It also labels lesion locations according to McDonald criteria automatically.

  • GitHub: github.com/CompImg/LST-AI
  • Paper (NeuroImage: Clinical 2024): ScienceDirect | PMC full text

Performance: Dice > 0.62 on MSSEG-1, outperforming classic LST, SAMSEG, and nnUNet.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Docker (recommended)pip (Linux) 
# Pull image (CPU/GPU)
docker pull jqmcginnis/lst-ai

# Run (replace paths with absolute paths)
docker run --rm \
  -v /path/to/input:/input \
  -v /path/to/output:/output \
  jqmcginnis/lst-ai \
  --t1 /input/t1.nii.gz \
  --flair /input/flair.nii.gz \
  --output /output

# Install HD-BET dependency first
git clone https://github.com/MIC-DKFZ/HD-BET
pip install -e HD-BET

# Install LST-AI
pip install lst-ai

Platform support

Native pip install is tested on Linux only. For macOS use Docker.

docs.title: FLAMeS — Fully Lesion-Aware MS Segmentation
docs.text:
FLAMeS is a robust deep learning model for automated MS lesion segmentation, designed to generalize across diverse MRI acquisition parameters and scanner types.

  • HuggingFace Space (interactive demo): huggingface.co/spaces/FrancescoLR/FLAMeS
  • Model weights: huggingface.co/FrancescoLR/FLAMeS-model
  • Paper (Journal of Neuroimaging, 2025): Wiley | PMC full text
  • Authors: Dereskewicz E, La Rosa F, dos Santos Silva J, et al.

docs.title: Quick Start via HuggingFace Space
docs.text:
The easiest way to try FLAMeS is the interactive web demo — no installation required:

  1. Go to huggingface.co/spaces/FrancescoLR/FLAMeS
  2. Upload your NIfTI file (T2/FLAIR brain MRI)
  3. Download the predicted lesion mask

HuggingFace Zero GPU

The Space runs on HuggingFace's free Zero GPU tier — it may take a minute to cold-start.

docs.title: Citation
docs.text:
@article{dereskewicz2025flames,
  title   = {FLAMeS: A robust deep learning model for automated multiple sclerosis lesion segmentation},
  author  = {Dereskewicz, E and La Rosa, F and dos Santos Silva, J and others},
  journal = {Journal of Neuroimaging},
  year    = {2025},
  doi     = {10.1111/jon.70085}
}

docs.title: Comparison
docs.text: Tool Input Platform DL Open source Best for LST (LGA) FLAIR + T1 MATLAB/SPM No Yes Classic pipeline, SPM users LST (LPA) FLAIR only MATLAB/SPM No Yes Quick single-modality run LST-AI FLAIR + T1 Linux / Docker Yes (U-Net) Yes Best accuracy, CLI/batch FLAMeS FLAIR Web / Python Yes Yes Quick demo, multi-site robustness

docs.title: Other Tools (for reference)
docs.text:
• CNN-MS Lesion Segmentation (Valverde) — Early CNN-based approach
  • ivadomed / MICCAI 2021 MS challenge — Framework used for new lesion detection
  • Consensus comparison 2024 (Scientific Reports) — Benchmark of multiple automated methods
